text: BLANCHVILLESKILL, a parish in co. Kilkenny, in the vicinity of Gowran. Area 854 acres. Pop. 152.

BLANCNEZ (CAPE), or CALAIS CLIFF, a conspicuous headland on the coast of France, 2 leagues W of Calais, and 2 leagues W by N from Cape Grinez. It is understood that the submarine telegraphic communication which it is proposed to lay down between the two coasts of the channel will be from the South Foreland, on the English coast, to Cape Grinez or 出面は重ね Cape Blancnez, on the French coast. The soundings between these points are gradual, varying from 7 fath. near the shore, to a maximum of 36 fath. in mid-channel.

BLANCO (CAPE), a promontory on the S coast B of the Morea, in N lat. 36° 44'.—Also a cape on the R SE coast of Sicily, in N lat. 35° 30'.—Also a cape on the N coast of Corsica, to the W of Cape Corso .- II. Also a promontory on the S coast of Corfu, in N 处 lat. 39° 30'.—Also a cape on the N coast of Spain, D) in the principality of Asturias, 32 m. E by S of Cape 8. Ortegal, in N lat. 43° 36'.—Also a promontory on なる the W coast of Ivica, one of the Pithyusæ islands, in N lat. 39° 1'.—Also a cape on the S coast of the island of Majorca, in N lat. 39° 21', forming the E と point of the bay of Palma, and 41 leagues SE of Cape Cala Figuera.—Also a promontory of Naples, 8 on the E coast of Calabria, in N lat. 38° 59'. 10
BLANCO (CAPE), a cape of Syria. See ABIAD 5 (RAS-EL).
BLANCO (CAPE), a cape on the SW coast of the bi. island of Cyprus, 9 m. SE of Baffa, in N lat. 34° 38'. 計画

BLANCO (CAPE), a cape on the W coast of Africa, in N lat. 20° 46′ 26″, W long. 17° 4′ 10″. は [Belcher]. It projects to a considerable distance into the sea, and except Cape Verd, forms the most westerly point of the African continent. It is a white cliff about 150 ft. high, rising vertically from a gentle slope which extends from its base to the water-edge. The peninsula of which it is the termination is a mixture of calcareous and siliceous sandstone; and the whole surface is covered with loose shells of all dimensions, and of the species generally found in the adjacent bay. [Geog. Journ. vol. ii., p. 300.] The coast abounds in mullet, cod, bream, and green turtle; but water is scarce. N and NE winds prevail throughout the year. The greatest rise of tide is 10 ft.

BLANCO (CAPE), or NORTH CAPE BLANCO, a white sandstone promontory on the coast of Marocco, in N lat. 33° 8', W long. 8° 38'. At 6 m. N of this cape, the hills rise gradually from the beach to 465 ft., the greatest elevation on the W shore of Marocco. ġ

BLANCO (CAPE), a cape on the coast of Peru, 120 m. W of Guayaquil, forming the S point of the gulf of Tumbez, or Guayaquil, in S lat. 4° 18'.—Also a cape of the prov. of Costa Rica, on Guatimala, in N lat. 9° 30'.—Also a cape on the coast of Caraccas, in Venezuela, in N lat. 10° 40'.—Also a cape on the coast of California, in N lat. 32°.—Also a cape on the NW coast of New Albion, in N lat. 43° 23'. It forms a conspicuous point, particularly when seen from the S, being formed by a round pile, on high perpendicular cliffs, some of which are white, and rise a considerable height above the level of the sea. -Also a low rugged tongue of land on the E side of Patagonia, in S lat. 47° 15′, 6½ m. SE of Cape Three points.

BLANCO, a river of La Plata, in the prov. of Tucuman, rising near Candalaria, and flowing SSE probably into the Salado, on the r. bank.-Also a river rising in the Gran Chaco, and flowing E to the Parana, into which it flows on the r. bank, 40 m. below Corrientes.
BLANDAIN, a commune and town of Belgium, in the dep. of Hainault, 45 m. W of Tournay. Pop.
BLANDFORD, a township in Hampden co., in the 2,746. state of Massachusetts, 110 m. W by S of Boston, on Westfield river. Pop. 1,427.
BLANDFORD, a township of Lower Canada, in the co. of Nicolet, stretching along the Becancour. Area 54,131 acres. BLA

BLANDFORD-FORUM, or CHIPPING-BLANDFORD, a market-town and parish in the co. of Dorset, 103 m. SW of London, and 48 m. of Popham-Beacons, on the Southampton railway. Area of p. 920 acres. Pop. 3,349. The town stands on a bend of the river Stower, near the ancient ford called by the Romans Trajectus Belaniensis, from which it takes its name. It has suffered frequently from fire; so that though an ancient town, the houses are in general comparatively modern. It was partially consumed in 1677 and 1713; but the most extensive conflagration took place in 1731, when the whole town, with the exception of 40 houses, was burnt to the ground. The houses are neatly built of brick; and the streets regular and well-paved. There are three bridges over the Stower. The town-hall is a handsome building in the Doric style. Races are held in August, on a fine race-course in the immediate vicinity of the town. - The manufacture of lace, which formerly flourished here, has declined; but a number of females receive employment in the manufacture of shirt-buttons, which is still considerable, though not so extensive as in former times. The corporate revenues, in 1837, amounted to about £200.—At the E end of the town are the remains of Damory court, the baronial mansion of the family of that name, who were anciently lords of the manor. Near this stood the famous Damory oak, which in 1747 was 75 ft. in height; the trunk was 23 ft. in diam. at the bottom, and a cavity in its centre was 15 ft. wide. - The duke of Marlborough takes the title of Marquis from this place.
BLANDFORD (St. MARY), a parish in Dorsetshire, on the Stower, opposite Blandford-Forum. Area 1,340 acres. Pop. 407.

BLANE, a stream in Stirlingshire, rising in the Lennox hills, and falling into the Endrick, near the influx of that river into Loch Lomond, after a course of 10 m. It forms several romantic 'spouts' or cascades.
BLANES, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, in the subdivision and 40 m. ENE of Barcelona, near the embouchure of the Tordera. Pop. 4,000.
BLANEY, a bay on the S side of Lower Lough Erne, in co. Fermanagh, 8 m. E by S of Belleek.

BLANGY, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Calvados. The cant. comprises 21 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,443. The com., 6 m. SE of Pont l'Eveque, has a pop. of 947.—Also a canton and commune in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, arrond. and 18 m. NE of Neufchatel. The cant., comprising 23 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 13,660. Pop. of com.
BLANIZ, a river of Bohemia, in the circle of Pra- 1,717. chin, which joins the Wottawa, on the r. bank, near Schremerberg.
BLANKENBERGHE, a village of Belgium, in W Flanders, 11 m. NW of Bruges, on the coast of the German ocean. Pop. 1,800. It has a small fishing-port.

BLANKENBURG, a principality and town of Brunswick, isolated between the prov. of Prussian Saxony and the prov. of Hanover. It has a superficial area of 196 sq. m., with a pop. of 22,000. The N part is flat and fertile; but the S is intersected by branches of the Hartz mountains, which are covered with dense forests, and contain iron-mines. The principal towns are B., Huttenrode, Hassenfelde, Braunlage, Wieda, and Hohegeiss.—This territory came into the possession of the house of Brunswick in 1599; and in 1707 was raised to the rank of a principality by Joseph I.—Its cap., of the same name, is situated on the Goldbach, at the foot of the Hartz, at an elevation of 780 ft. above sea-level, 7 m. W of Quedlinburg. Pop. 3,500. On a hill adjoining the town is the chateau of the princes of B., in which Louis resided from 1796 to 1798, as Count Von Lille.

BLANKENBURG, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, in the gov. of Cologne, on the Sieg. Pop. 350.—Also a village of Schwarzburg- Rudolstadt, 4 m. SW of Rudolstadt, on the Rinne, near its junction with the Schwarze. Pop. 1,200. It has manufactories of paper and leather.—Also a hamlet of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, in the Upper Simmenthal, near Bettelried, at an alt. of 3,300 ft. above sea-level.

BLANKENESE, a town of Denmark, in Holstein, on the r. bank of the Elbe, 9 m. W of Hamburg. Pop. 2,500, chiefly engaged in the fisheries and coasting-trade. There is a ferry to Crantz, in Bremen, across the Elbe, which here unites its several arms in a single channel.
BLANKENHAIN, a village of Saxony, in the bailiwick and 16 m. NW of Zwickau. Pop. 557.- Also a town of Saxe-Weimar, 8 m. S of Weimar. Pop. 1,450. It has manufactures of porcelain and pottery-ware.
BLANKENHAM, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Overyssell, 12 m. WSW of Steenwyk, on the Zuyder-zee. Pop. 1,000. It has a small port, and carries on a trade in butter.
BLANKENHEIM, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, in the gov. and 32 m. SE of Aixla-Chapelle. Pop. 600.
BLANKENLOCH, a town of Baden, in the circle of the Middle Rhine, on the Pfinz river. Pop. 1,217. The grand-duke has a horse-stud here.
BLANKENSTEIN, a town of Prussia, in Westphalia, in the gov. of Arnsberg, on the l. bank of the Ruhr, 6 m. S of Bochun. Pop, 1,000.
BLANKERATH, a village of Prussia, in the Rhine prov., in the gov. of Coblentz, circle of Zell. Copper and lead are wrought in the neighbourhood.
BLANKNEY WITH LINWOOD, a parish in Lincolnshire, 10 m. N of Sleaford. Area 6,000 acres. Pop. 640.
BLANKSTATTEN, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Middle Franconia, 3 m. N of Beilngries, on the Salz. Pop. 300. A rich Benedictine abbey formerly existed here.

BLANQUEFORT, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, arrond. of Bordeaux. The cant. comprises 9 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,915. The com., 5 m. NW of Bordeaux, has a pop. of 2,074. Excellent white wines are grown here.—Also a commune in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, cant. of Fumel. Pop. 1,760.

BLANQUILLA, an island, or rather the extremity of a low point of land off the NE coast of Yucatan, in N lat. 21° 26'.—Also a low sandy islet, forming the W entrance of the port of Vera Cruz, in N lat. 19° 5′, W long. 95° 58′.
BLANQUILLA. See BLANCA. BLANSKŒ, a town of Austria, in Moravia, in the circle and 12 m. N of Brunn, on the Zwittawa. Pop. 1,453. There are important iron-mines, furnaces, and foundries here.

BLANTYRE, a parish and village in Lanarkshire, stretching along the S bank of the Clyde, 8 m. SE of Glasgow, and 3 m. W of Hamilton. Area 3,300 Scots acres. Pop. 3,047, of whom 1,464 were in the v. of Blantyre-works, and 261 in the Kirktown of B. Ironstone and limestone are wrought in the p.; and there are very extensive cotton-spinning and dyeing works which were founded in 1785. The other villages in the p. are Auchinraith, Auchintiber, Barnhill, Hunthill, and Stonefield.
BLANZAC, a canton and commune of France, in BLA cant., comprising 20 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 11,220.—The com. is 14 m. SSW of Angouleme, on the Nay. Pop. 643.
BLANZAIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vienne, cant. of Civray. Pop. 1,580.
BLANZAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Clermont. Pop. 1,380.

BLANZY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Montcenis. Pop. 2,664. Coal is extensively wrought here. The quantity raised in 1832 amounted to 800,000 hectolitres, or 2,269,600 bushels.
BLARINGHEM, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. and 6 m. WSW of Hazebrouck. Pop. 1,800.

BLARNEY, a village in the p. of Garrycloyne, co. Cork, on a rivulet of its own name, 31 m. NW of Cork. Pop. in 1831, 417; in 1841, 253. Previous to 1765, it consisted merely of a few mud cabins; but immediately afterwards, it began to assume a town-character of pretension, which it has not kept up. Yet a woollen manufactory and a paper-mill in the vicinity are still at work. B. is celebrated for its absurdly wondrous "stone,"-its prominent figure in light literature,-its ancient castle,-and the great beauty of its environs. The stone has long been famed for imparting, by a kiss, the faculty of talking "blarney," a word which Miss Plumtre translates "rhodomontade." When or how the stone acquired its singular reputation is not correctly ascertained; yet the adoption of the word "blarney," as a slang term, is supposed to have originated in Lord Clancarty's often promising, when the prisoner of Sir George Carew, to surrender his strong castle of B. to the soldiers of the Queen, and as often inventing some smooth and palatable excuse for exonerating himself from his promise. Even the position and the identity of the stone are matter of doubt: all parties agree that it is somewhere in the castle, but even the local guides or ciceroni disagree among themselves as to its being at the summit, at a considerable elevation, at the base, or detachedly on the ground!—The principal piece of light literature in which B. figures is the popular song of "The groves of Blarney," which is satisfactorily ascertained to have been written in 1798, by R. A. Milliken, a Cork attorney. The castle of B. consists of a mansion of modern construction, and of part of an ancient castellated structure. The ancient pile was built about the middle of the 15th cent.
BLARS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot, cant. of Lauzes. Pop. 536.

BLAS (SAN), a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Salisco, at the embouchure of the Rio Santiago, in N lat. 21° 32′ 24", W long. 105° 18′ 27". The town is perched, like an eagle's nest, on the top of a rock 150 ft. high, absolutely precipitous on three sides, and very steep on the fourth, and rising out of a low swampy plain which, in the rainy season, is laid completely under water, and is overflowed to a considerable extent at spring-tides. From December to May, the sky is clear, land and sea-breezes prevail, and there is little sickness; but from June to November the heat is greatly increased, and excessive rains deluge the country; so that all who can abandon the place in May, - "rich and poor stream off indiscriminately together;" and the pop., which in the fine season used to amount to 3,000, is reduced to about 150. It was, from 1812 to 1821, the only port in the whole Mexican territory, besides Vera Cruz, open to foreign trade; but it has been nearly abandoned as a port in consequence of its natural inferiority to Mazatlan and Guaymas.

BLAS (CAPE SAN), the S point of a long tongue the dep. of Charente, arrond. of Angouleme.—The of land that stretches off 5 or 6 m. from the main in the gulf of Mexico, in N lat. 29° 38', W long. 85° 23'. All the coast from the Mississippi to this cape is low land, and is bordered by a bank of soundings, the edge of which extends to N lat. 28° 50'.

BLAS (GULF OF SAN), a gulf on the coast of Columbia, of which the NE extremity is formed by Point San Blas, in N lat. 9° 34' 36", W long. 79° 1' 24". There is a small settlement of Indians established here for the purpose of taking turtle. The quantity of tortoise-shell procured by them amounts on an average to 15,000 lbs. each year, of the annual value of from £28,000 to £32,000.
BLASENDORF. See BALASFALVA.
BLASERN, a village in Prussian Saxony, in the gov. of Merseburg, SW of Wittenberg. Pop. 176.
BLASEWITZ, a village of Saxony, 2 m. E of Dresden, on the l. bank of the Elbe. Pop. 220.
BLASIEN (SANCT), a village and bailiwick of Baden, in the circle of the Upper Rhine, 20 m. SE of Friburg, on the Alb, in a valley of the Black forest. Pop. 948.

BLASKETS, or FERRITER ISLANDS, a cluster of islands in co. Kerry, off the extremity of the Corkaguiney peninsula, between Dingle bay and Smerwick ď. harbour. The Great Blasket, the largest of the group, is about <math>2\frac{1}{2}</math> m. long, and lies about <math>\frac{3}{4}</math> of a m. W of Dunmore head. Inishnubro lies \(\frac{3}{4}\) of a m. SW, Inishmacilaan 13 m. SSW, the Foze Rock 31 m. SW, Tiraght <math>2\frac{3}{4}</math> m. W by N, Inishtuiskan <math>2\frac{1}{2}</math> m. NNW, and Beginish half-a-mile NNE of the Great Blasket; Į. but some of these, as well as others which we have not named, are mere rocks, while none measure more than about 2 m. in circumference. The group, as to general character, presents stupendous and frowning cliffs; and the scenery of the isles, during a storm from the W, borders on the terrific. Innumerable flocks of sea-fowl frequent them; among these is a delicious bird, called in Irish gourdet, resembling the ortolan, and said to be peculiar to the Blaskets.
BLASTON (St. MICHAEL), a parish in Leicestershire, 7 m. NE of Market-Harborough. Area 1,300 acres. Pop. 102.
BLASZI, a town of Poland, in the obwod and 18 m. SE of Kalisch. Pop. 1,500.

BLATCHINGTON (East and West), two parishes in Sussex; the former 11 m. E by S of Brighton; the latter 21 m. NW by W of Brighton. Area of East B. 2,110 acres. Pop. 163. Area of West B. 590 acres. Pop. 64.
BLATCHINWORTH, a township in the p. of Rochdale, 4 m. NE of Rochdale. Pop., with Calderbrook, 4,456, who are chiefly employed in the manufacture of woollens.
BLATHERWYCKE, a parish in Northamptonshire, 8 m. N by E of Rockingham. Area 2,750 acres. Pop. 236.
BLATNA, or BLATNO, a town of Bohemia, in the Prachin circle, 20 m. NW of Pisek. Pop. 1,805. There is a fine seignorial castle of the 14th cent. here.
BLATNICZA, a mountain-range of Bosnia, in the sanjak of Bosnaserai, a branch of the Balkan chain.
BLATNON, a village of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 20 m. SE of Tournay. Pop. 2,319. There are woollen manufactories here.
BLAUBERG, a hill in the Russian gov. of Cournear Ulm. land, on the coast of the gulf of Riga. It commands an extensive view over the plains of Livonia.

BLAUBEUREN, or BLAUBAIERN, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, on the Blau river, 10 m. W of Ulm, at an elevation of 1,770 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,965. It has manufactories of linen and woollen stuffs. BLE
BLAUEN, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Upper Rhine, near the Zell river.—Also a mountain in the Black forest, 3,586 German ft. in height.
BLAUFELDEN, a town of Wurtemberg, in the Jaxt circle, 4 m. NNE of Gerabronn, at an alt. of 1,530 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 907, Protestants.
BLAVA (CAPE), a headland forming the NE point of the island of Lemnos, in the Grecian archipelago, in N lat. 40° 2′, E long. 25° 28′.

BLAVET, a river of France, rising in an etang of the same name, 8 m. W of Rostrenen, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, and flowing into the Atlantic at Port Louis, in the dep. of Morbihan, after a course of 120 m., in which it passes Pontivy, Hennebon, and Lorient. It is navigable to Hennebon, a distance of 10 m. Between Hennebon and Pontivy it has been converted into a canal, which forms a branch of the canal navigation from Nantes to Brest.
BLAWITH, a chapelry in Lancashire, 7 m. N of Ulverstone, on the W bank of the Crahe. Area 2,620 acres. Pop. 178.
BLAXHALL, a parish in Suffolk, 4 m. E of Market-Wykeham. Area 1,930 acres. Pop. 576.
BLAXTON, a township in the p. of Fenningley, W. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. NE of Bawtry. Area 1,640 acres. Pop. 183.

BLAYE, or BLAIE, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Gironde. -The arrond. comprises an area of 72,347 hectares, and contains 4 cant., viz.: B., Bourg-sur-Gironde, Saint-Ciersla-Lande, and Saint-Savin. Pop. in 1831, 56,406; in 1836, 55,460; in 1846, 58,723.—The cant. comprises 13 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 14,438.—The town stands on the r. bank of the Gironde, 20 m. NNW of Bordeaux. Pop. of com. in 1784, 3,042; in 1836, 3,801; in 1846, 4,101. It consists of two divisions, viz.: the higher town, or citadel, situated on a rocky eminence; and the lower or commercial portion of the town. The latter has a theatre and a fine public fountain. A royal hydrographical school, a chamber of commerce, and an agricultural society, exist here. On a small island in the middle of the river-here 6 m. in breadth-is Fort du Paté, and, on the opposite or l. side, Fort Medoc. The port, or roadsted, is much frequented. B. is an important pilot station, and has extensive building docks. The principal exports are wine, brandy, oil, apples, nuts, rosin, soap, and timber. Fairs are held several times a-year, the principal occurs on the 25th of November.
BLAYMARD, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Lozere, arrond. of Mende.-The cant. comprises 11 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 6,819.—The village is 12 m. E of Mende. Pop. 583.
BLAYNEY, a parcel in Llanfihangel-Cwmdu p., in Breconshire. Pop. 583.
BLAYNE, a hamlet in the p. of Llandebie, Carmarthenshire. Pop. with Glyntay, 1,061.
BLAZE (Mount), a low sand hill on the coast of Australia, in S lat. 20°, E long. 119° 40'. Behind it the country is swampy, with mangroves, for a few miles; and then gradually rises.
BLAZEY (St.), a parish in Cornwall, 4 m. NE of St. Austell. Area 2,000 acres. Pop. 3,234.
BLAZIMONT, a commune and town of France, Blautopf, near Blaubaiern, and flows into the Danube in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Sauveterre, 18 m.
BLAZOW, a town of Galicia, in the circle and 38 m. NW of Zolkiew. Pop. 500.
BLAZOWA, a town of Galicia, cap. of a seignory of the same name, in the circle and 30 m. ENE of Jaslo, and 10 m. SSE of Rzeszow, on an affluent of the Wislok. It contains a castle.
BLEADON, a parish in Somersetshire, 6 m. WNW of Axbridge, on the N bank of the Axe. Area 2,490 acres. Pop. 778. It comprises the hamlets of Oldmixton and Shiplet.
BLEAN, a parish in Kent, 2 m. NW by N of Canterbury. Area 5,760 acres. Pop. 606.
BLEASBY, a parish in Nottinghamshire, 4 m. SSE of Southwell, on the W bank of the Trent. Area 1,550 acres. Pop. 353.—Also a hamlet in the p. of Legsby, in Lincolnshire. Pop. 123.
BLEASDALE, a chapelry in Lancaster p., in Lancashire, 4 m. E by N of Garstang. Area 8,090 acres. Pop. 249.
BLEATARN, a hamlet in the p. of Warcop, in Westmoreland. Pop. in 1821, 129. Wordsworth has made the sequestered vale in which this hamlet is situated the abode of his 'Solitary.'
BLECHINGDON. See BLETCHINGTON.

BLECKEDE, or BLEKEDE, a town of Hanover, the cap. of the bailiwick of Bleckede and Gartze, in the principality and 15 m. ENE of Luneburg, on the 1. bank of the Elbe, 19 m. NW of Dannenburg. Pop. 736. It contains a custom-house, and an old castle. The river in the vicinity affords excellent fishing. Pop. of bail. 8,272.
BLEDAS, an islet of the Mediterranean, in the Balearic group, near the N coast of Minorca.
BLEDDFFA, or Blethvaugh, a parish in Radnorshire, 6 m. SW by S of Knighton. Pop. 235.
BLEDDINGTON, a parish in Gloucestershire, 4 m. SE of Stow-on-the-Wold. Area 1,110 acres. Pop. 354.
BLEDLOW, a parish in Bucks, 51 m. ESE of Thame. Area 4,130 acres. Pop. 1,205.

BLEDSOE, a county of Tennessee, U. S., in the SE part of the state. It comprises a superficial area of 500 sq. m.; and is intersected by the Sequatchee. It is generally mountainous. The cap. is Pikeville. Pop. in 1830, 4,648; in 1840, 5,676.
BLEEDA. See BLIDAH.
BLEEFJELE, a mountain-ridge of Norway, in the district of Buskerud, on the borders of Bradsberg.
BLEESERN, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony, and circle of Wittenberg. It contains a royal stud.

BLEGEH-UULE, a village of Denmark, in the prov. of Jutland, in the bailiwick and 8 m. NW of Tisted, on a small lake, 3 m. from the shore of the Skager-rack. BLEGNO, BLENIO, BREGNO, OF BRENNO, [GERMAN, Bellenzer, or Polenzerthal, a valley and district of Switzerland, in the cant. of Tessin. It extends in a S direction from the summit of La Greina; and is watered by a river of the same name, which unites with the Tessin, a mile to the NW of Osogna. Its length is about 24 m., and its average breadth 1 m. It produces grain, wine, and fruit, in abundance; and cattle in immense numbers are fed on its pastures. The mountains contain mines of lead and copper; and at Dongio there is a mineral spring. In 1512, an extensive land-slip, obstructing the course of the Blegno, converted the valley into an immense lake. On the irruption of its waters, two years subsequently, they destroyed in their devastating career 600 persons, and swept away the bridges at Bellinzone. This valley, after having successively belonged to the dukes of Milan, the Pepoli of Bologna, and the house of Bentivoglio, was placed, in 1500, under the protection of the cantons of Uri, Schwyz, and Unterwalden, and formed a bailiwick dependent upon and in the jurisdiction of these cant. Since 1798 it has formed a portion of the cant. of Tessin.—The dist. comprises 3 circles, viz.: Castro, Malvaglia, and Olivone; which are subdivided into 18 parishes. Pop. in 1836, 8,044. The cap. is Lotigna. The higher classes speak Italian; but the vernacular tongue is a dialect of the Grisons. BLE 780

BLEIBERG, or Bleyberg, [i. e., 'Lead mountain,'] a mountain and a village of Austria, in the prov. of Carinthia, in the circle and 8 m. W of Villach, and 67 m. NNW of Trieste. The v. or rather series of villages, are on an affluent of the Drave, at an alt. of 2,560 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 3,000. Quarries of marble, and mines of calamine, copper, and lead, exist here and in the adjoining Erzberg. The lead mines are the richest in Austria. Their annual produce is estimated at 16,000 tons.
BLEIBURG, or BLEYBURG, a town of Austria, in the prov. of Carinthia, in the circle and 25 m. E of Klagenfurt. There are iron works here.

BLEICHERODE, a town of Saxony, in the gov. and 39 m. NW of Erfurth, in the circle and 11 m. WSW of Nordhausen, between the Bode and the Wipper. It contains manufactories of woollen fabrics and oil-mills, and has an extensive trade in coarse linens. In the environs are mineral springs. Pop. in 1837, 2,462.
BLEINE, a hamlet in Llangendeirnie p.. in Carmarthenshire. Pop. with Llan, 634.
BLEISTADT, or BLEYSTADT, a town of Bohemia, in the circle and 8 m. WNW of Elnbogen, on the Vogtland. In the environs are mines of lead and garnets.
BLEISTEIN, or Pleistein, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Ober-Pfalz, on the r. bank of the Pfreimt, 12 m. ESE of Weiden. Pop. 932. It contains iron-works; and in the environs are some lead mines.
BLEISWYK, a village of Holland, in the prov. of S Holland and cant. of Delft, on the canal and 7 m. NNE of Rotterdam. Pop. 1,130.
BLEKING, or Blekingen. See Carlskrona.
BLENCOGO, a township in the p. of Broomfield, in Cumberland, 5 m. W by S of Wigton. Pop. 211.

BLENCOW (GREAT and LITTLE), two townships in Cumberland; the former in the p. of Dacre, 5 m. WNW of Penrith; the latter in the p. of Greystoke, 5 m. W of Penrith. Pop. of Great B., 64; of Little B., 69.
BLENDECQUES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of St. Omer. Pop. 1,406.
BLENDON, a hamlet in Bexley p., in Kent. Pop. 122.
BLENDWORTH, a parish in Hamps, 7 m. SSE of Petersfield. Area 3,110 acres. Pop. 280.

BLENEAU, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Yonne, arrond. of Ivigny.- The cant. comprises 8 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 6,774.—The town and com. is 32 m. WSW of Auxerre. Pop. 1,278. It has an extensive commerce in timber. The victory of Marshal Turenne over the prince of Conde, in 1652, took place here.
BLENERVILLE, a village and port in the p. of Annagh, co. Kerry, 1 m. WSW of Tralee. Pop. 225. A considerable trade is conducted in the export of corn to Liverpool.
BLENHAM, a town in Shoharie co., in the state of New York, U. S. Pop. 2,725.

BLENHEIM, an extra-parochial hamlet, locally situated in the p. of Woodstock, co. of Oxford; 621 m. WNW of London. B. castle was founded in 1704, the demesne having been conferred on John Churchill, duke of Marlborough, by Queen Anne, and the sum of £500,000 having been voted by parliament for the erection of a suitable mansion, as a permanent testimony of the national gratitude. The architect was Sir John Vanburgh, and the castle was called after a German village—the subject of the next article-near which the duke obtained a signal victory over the French and Bavarians, on the 2d of Aug., 1704. The building is in a splendid, though not faultless style of architecture, and measures 348 ft. from wing to wing. The interior is magnificent, especially the hall, the walls of which are 67 ft. in height; the roof, which is supported by Corinthian pillars, was painted by Sir James Thornhill. The library occupies the entire W front; and is 200 ft. long, by 32 ft. wide in the centre. The grounds are extensive and laid out with great beauty and picturesque effect. They contain a triumphal arch, a pillar 130 ft. high supporting a colossal statue of the duke, a fountain copied from that of the Piazza Navona at Rome, and an artificial lake supplied by the small river Glyme. The ancient palace of Woodstock formerly stood in this park.

BLENHEIM, or BLINDHEIM, a village in the circle of the Upper Danube, in Bavaria, on the Danube, near Hochstädt. Pop. of the v. and environs 1,500. Here was fought, Aug. 13, 1704, the famous battle of Blenheim, or, as it is more commonly called on the continent, the battle of Hochstädt. The French and Bavarian armies were drawn into an engagement under unfavourable circumstances. Both these armies were posted, under the command of Tallard, Marsin, and the elector of Bavaria, between the village of B. and that of Kinzingen, behind the Nebelbach, a small stream emptying into the Danube, which was on their right flank. They amounted to 56,000 men, whilst the forces of Marlborough and Eugene were about 52,000. The first had thrown their troops chiefly into the two villages, which they considered as points of support for their wings, though they were at too great a distance in front of their main position. They so little expected to be attacked, that when the line of the allies began to move, at 2 o'clock in the morning, they supposed them to be marching off. Even at 7 o'clock, when the heads of the eight columns, with which Eugene and Marlborough advanced towards the Nebelbach, were seen, Tallard thought the whole a stratagem intended to cover the retreat; but he soon saw his error. The French and Bavarians made every exertion to prevent the passage of the enemy over the Nebelbach, and the capture of the two villages, the conquest of which was considered by Marlborough and Eugene as decisive. Their line of attack was uncommonly long, being about 41 m. Marlborough, in order to secure his r. wing, attacked B., but without success; he then changed his plan, and threw himself, with his principal forces, into the wide interval between the right wing and the centre of the enemy, leaving only as many troops before B. as were necessary to check the body which occupied this position. At 5 o'clock in the afternoon, he succeeded in passing the Nebelbach, by which his victory was decided. The French, in the centre, were obliged to retreat; and their example was followed by the Bavarians on the left wing, who for a long time had resisted the impetuous attacks of Prince Eugene. Mariborough, instead of pursuing the retreating enemy, placed himself between the line of retreat and the position of B., guarded by 18,000 men, who were thus cut off from assistance, and forced to surrender. The cavalry was routed by the fire of the English cannon and musketry; and a large part of the defeated army remained dead on the field of battle-which was covered with more than 11,000 corpses—or were made prisoners. Tallard himself was among the prisoners; his son was killed. The consequences of the battle were decisive. Bavaria, as Marlborough had anticipated, fell into the power of Austria. Fortune deserted Louis as it did Napoleon after the battle of Leipsic; and, though he was able to continue the war for nearly ten years longer, it was only owing to the dissensions among the allies themselves, who contended about the best use to be made of their victory till the opportunity to use it was lost.
BLENHEIM, a township of Upper Canada, in the Gore district, intersected by an affluent of the Ouse.
BLENIO. See Blegno.
BLENNERHASSET, a township in the p. of Torpenhow, in Cumberland, 7 m. SW of Wigton, on the S bank of the Ellen. Pop. with Kirkland, 224.

BLE'NOD-LES-TOUL, or BLENOD-AUX-OIGNONS, a commune of France, in the dep of Meurthe, in the cant. and 6 m. SSW of Toul, on a brook between two hills. It contains a handsome church, an antique statue of Apollo, and the ruins of an ancient temple. Pop. 1,511.
BLENYON, a town of the island of Banka, in the Asiatic archipelago, on a river of the same name, near the N coast. In the environs are extensive tin-mines. Pop., Chinese and Malay, 170.

BLEONE, a river of France, in the dep. of the Lower Alps, which takes its rise in the mountain of BLE Sestrieris, to the NW of Allos, runs SW, passes Digne, and after a course of 39 m., falls into the l. bank of the Durance, 1 m. N of Les Meès. Its chief tributary is the Bees.

BLE'RANCOURT, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Coucy-le- Château, 24 m. W of Laon. It possesses an extensive commerce in grain, hemp, and horses. Pop. 1,185. It was the birth-place of the celebrated Conventionalist, Saint Just.

BLERE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, arrond. of Tours. The cant. comprises 15 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 14,694. It produces good wine.—The town is situated on the l. bank of the Cher, 18 m. ESE of Tours. It was formerly garrisoned. In the vicinity is the castle of Chenonceaux, celebrated as the residence of Diana of Poitiers. Pop. 2,948.

BLESEN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. and regency of Posen, in the circle of Birbaum, on the 1. bank of the Obra, 9 m. NW of Meseritz. It contains a Benedictine convent. Pop., consisting of Germans and Poles, 1,179. It has some breweries and brandy distilleries.

BLESLE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Haute-Loire, arrond. of Brioude. The cant. comprises 11 com., and contained in 1831, a pop. of 6,192. The town is 12 m. W of Brioude. Pop. 1,621.

BLESSINGTON, a parish and town in co. Wicklow. Area 15,780 acres. Pop. 1,702. The Liffey has its rise in this p., at an alt. of 1,600 ft. above sea-level. The central and E districts present several lofty mountains, amongst which are Gravall 2,352 ft., Duff 2,364 ft., Sorrel-hill 1,915 ft., Blackmoor 1,464 ft., Glenflugh 1,327 ft., and Ballinatona 1,346 ft.—The town of B. is situated on the Liffey, 151 m. N by E of Baltinglass. Pop. 1,466. It gave the title of Earl to the family of Gardner, which became extinct in 1829. The countess of the last earl is well-known as one of the fashionable novelists of
BLET, a commune and town of France, in the the day. dep. of Cher, and cant. of Nerondes, 22 m. SE of Bourges. Pop. 1,063.

BLETCHINGLY, or BLECHINGLY, a borough and parish in the co. of Surrey, 5 m. E of Reigate. Area of p. 5,250 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,344; in 1841, 3,546. The town is situated near the chalk hills which divide the county, on an eminence commanding a view of the South Downs. B. returned two members to parliament since the 23° Edward I.; but being all the property of one individual, he returned the members. It was of course wholly 'cashiered' by the reform bill; and immediately sunk into such insignificance as to be brought to the hammer in September, 1835, when upwards of 100 dwelling-houses were sold for £11,000.
BLETCHINGTON, a parish in Oxfordshire, in Ploughley hundred. Area 2,540 acres. Pop. 638.
BLETCHLEY, a parish in Bucks, 2 m. W of Fenny-Stratford. Area 2,220 acres. Pop. 685.—Also a township in Moretonsay p., in Salop. Pop. 101.
BLETHERSTON, a parish in Pembrokeshire, 5 m. WNW of Narberth. Pop. 271.
BLETSOE, a parish in Bedfordshire, 6 m. NNW of Bedford. Area 2,220 acres. Pop. 420.

BLETTERANS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Jura, arrond. of Lons-le- Saunier. The cant. comprises 12 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 10,998. The town is 8 m. NW of Lons-le-Saunier. It has some manufactories of pottery, and a considerable trade in grain. Pop.

BLEURIE, a river of Lower Canada, which rises 1,185. in a lake to the S of De Lery, and running S, falls into Jackson's creek, nearly opposite Isle aux Noix, in the river Richelieu.—Also a seignory in Rouville co., on the E bank of the Richelieu, and bounded on the NE by Chambly and Monnoir; and on the S by Sabrevois. It is of a triangular form, and is generally low and swampy. It contains, however, some good timber and near the river some tracts of fine cultivation.
BLEVAINCOURT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vosges, and com. of Lamarche. Pop. 467.
BLEVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, and cant. of Ingouville. It contains ferruginous mineral springs. Pop. 1,160.

BLEVIO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, in the deleg. and near the lake of Como. The environs are adorned with many handsome villas, amongst which are the Villa d'Este, formerly Villa-Garruo.
BLEVY, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Eure-et-Loire, cant. of Chateauneuf-en- Thymerais, 11 m. SW of Dreux. Pop. 729.
BLEWBERRY, a parish in Berks, 4 m. NE of East Ilsley. Area 6,950 acres. Pop. 1,096.

BLEWFIELDS, or BLUEFIELDS, the capital of the Mosquito territory, in Central America, on a bay of the E coast, near the mouth of the river of the same name. It has an excellent harbour, composed of two lagunes, the lower of which is 20 m. in length, and full of kays or islets; but the upper, which is not more than a mile broad at its entrance, increases in width to 5 or 6 m., and has a general depth of 3 and 31 fath.—The B. river, called also Escondito, takes its rise in the mountains of Segovia, 40 m. S of the town of that name, and after a course of about 250 m. in an ESE direction, falls into the Caribbean sea in N lat. 11° 55'. It receives the Little Mahogany, and Mahogany creeks, and the Escondito, on the l.; and the Ramas creek on the r. The Cookra and Woolwa tribes, settled on its banks in the interior, are a quiet race; and on good terms with those on the Ramas, and with the pop. on the lagunes. The lands bordering on the river are rich and fertile, capable of growing cotton, coffee, sugar, and cocoa.
BLEXEN, a town of Western Germany, in the duchy of Oldenburg, on the estuary of the Weser, 30 m. NNE of Oldenburg. Pop. 387.
BLEY, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Haute- Saone, cant. of Autrey. It contains some iron works. Pop. 20.
BLEYBURG. See BLEIBURG.
BLEYENBACH, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. and 22 m. NE of Bern, 5 m. SSW of Aarwangen.
BLICKLING, a parish in Norfolk, 14 m. NW of Aylsham. Area 2,240 acres. Pop. 356.
BLICQUY, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 22 m. NW of Mons. It has some woollen manufactories. Pop. 1,757.

BLIDAH, or Belidah, a town of Algiers, in the prov. and 36 m. SSW of Algiers, and 24 m. N of Mediah; in N lat. 36° 60', on an affluent of the Marsa-Zafran, or Massafran, at the base of the Little Atlas, 100 metres or 328 ft. above the level of the Massafran, and 185 metres or 607 ft. above sea-level. It occupies the site of a town of the same name, which was destroyed by an earthquake in 1825; and is defended by a wall 12 ft. in height, and fortifications. B. was taken by the French in Nov. 1830; and has been regularly occupied by them since 1838. Its pop. in 1847 amounted to 7,487, of whom 3,985 were Europeans. Barracks have been erected; and a large hospital.—In the new French division of the BLI B., the district of Buffarik, and the district of Coleah. The district of B. is divided into the 5 com. of B., Joinville, Montpensier, Dalmatia, and Beni-Mered. The surrounding district is remarkably fertile, producing grain, and fruits in great abundance.
BLIDO, an island of the Baltic, near the E coast of Sweden, in N lat. 59° 37'.

BLIDWORTH, a parish in Nottinghamshire, 5 m. SE of Mansfield. Area 6,610 acres. Pop. 1,132. BLIES, BLIESE, or BLISE, a river of Western Germany, which takes its rise in the duchy of Saxe- Coburg, to the N of St. Wendel; passes the towns of Ottweiler and Neunkirchen in the Prussian prov. of the Lower Rhine, and Blieskastell in the Bavarian circle of the Rhine; and, after a tortuous course to the W, unites with the Sarre about 1 m. N of Sarreguemines, in the French dep. of the Moselle. Its principal affluents are the Osterbach and Erbach; and its total course 63 m., of which 54 m. are floatable.

BLIESKASTELL, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Pfalz, on the Blies, 5 m. W of Zwey- Brucken, or Deux-Ponts. It has some manufactories of tobacco, and contains a castle. Pop. 1,792.- A battle between the French and Prussians was fought here in 1793.
BLIGH (CAPE), a headland of Mywolla island, in the Fiji group, in the S Pacific.

BLIGH ISLAND, a small island of the N Pacific, near the S coast of Russian America, in Prince William's sound, in N lat. 60° 30', and W long. 148°. -Also an island in the S Pacific, in the Society group, in S lat. 21° 50′, and W long. 140° 50′.
BLIGNY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aube, cant. of Vendeuvre. It contains some glassworks. Pop. 824.

BLIGNY-SUR-OUCHE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Cote d'Or, arrond. of Beaune. The cant. comprises 23 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 8,046. The town is 10 m. NW of Beaune. Pop. 1,254. It contains several tanneries and hat-manufactories, and has a considerable commerce in grain, linen, leather, and cattle. Fairs are held here six times in the year.
BLIMONT (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Somme, cant. of Saint-Valery-sur-Somme. Pop. 1,246.
